content.text: Release Train Cadence and Planning Rituals

is a fixed, calendar-driven delivery vehicle: every N weeks a train departs to production regardless of which features made the cut. The cadence creates predictability for downstream consumers (model APIs, partner integrations, support teams) and forces scope decisions rather than endless slip.
The train leaves on time. Features that aren't ready wait for the next one — the schedule does not wait for features.
This sub-subtopic covers the ceremonies, artefacts, and tooling that keep a two-week release train running reliably for a Claude-based product.

attrs.alt: Release train cadence diagram showing overlapping two-week trains

content.text: Why Fixed Cadence Over Feature-Driven Releases

Feature-driven releases optimise for individual feature readiness but produce chaotic delivery patterns: irregular deploy windows, unpredictable support load spikes, and test debt that accumulates between 'big bang' releases. Fixed cadence solves this by decoupling the release rhythm from any individual feature:
— on-call knows a release is happening every other Tuesday, not 'whenever the feature is done'
— the cut-line meeting forces genuine prioritisation rather than scope creep
— Train N+1 begins planning while Train N is in its freeze phase (illustrated above)
— even if a feature slips one train, it ships two weeks later instead of waiting months
Each train follows the same repeating ceremony sequence. Adjust day-of-week to match your team's rhythm, but preserve the ordering:
Tooling: Automating Train State in GitHub
A GitHub milestone per train tracks scope commitment mechanically. The following workflow creates the milestone, pins the train branch, and labels issues automatically:

# .github/workflows/train-setup.yml
name: Setup Release Train

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      train_number:
        description: 'Train number (e.g. 42)'
        required: true
      due_date:
        description: 'Deploy date YYYY-MM-DD'
        required: true

jobs:
  setup:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Create milestone
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          gh api repos/${{ github.repository }}/milestones \
            -f title="Train ${{ inputs.train_number }}" \
            -f due_on="${{ inputs.due_date }}T00:00:00Z" \
            -f description="Release train ${{ inputs.train_number }}"

      - name: Create release branch
        run: |
          git checkout -b release/train-${{ inputs.train_number }}
          git push origin release/train-${{ inputs.train_number }}

      - name: Post Slack announcement
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_RELEASE_WEBHOOK }}
        run: |
          curl -s -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK" \
            -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
            -d '{
              "text": ":train: *Train ${{ inputs.train_number }}* is open. Deploy: ${{ inputs.due_date }}. Add PRs to the milestone."
            }'
Cut-Line Automation: The Scope Gate Script
On the Thursday code freeze, an automated script labels every open PR as either 'in-train' or 'missed-train' based on milestone assignment, and posts a summary to Slack:
Each train kick-off produces a structured scope document committed to the repository. This serves as the single source of truth during the sprint and feeds the post-deploy retro:

# Train 42 — Kick-off Scope

**Deploy target:** 2025-08-12 (Tuesday)
**Branch:** `release/train-42`
**On-call:** @alice (primary), @bob (secondary)

## Committed scope

| Feature | Owner | Risk | Notes |
|---------|-------|------|-------|
| Streaming response caching | @carol | Low | A/B flag behind `cache_streaming` |
| Model version upgrade to claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 | @dave | Medium | Canary 10% → 50% → 100% |
| Token usage dashboard | @eve | Low | Read-only, no API changes |

## Cut candidates (if capacity drops)

- Retry budget telemetry (moves to Train 43)
- Admin role self-service (deprioritised by PM)

## Risk register

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|
| Haiku upgrade latency regression | Automated P99 alert at >500ms; auto-rollback |
| Cache invalidation on model change | Flush cache namespace on deploy |

## Definition of done (per feature)

- [ ] Unit tests pass, coverage ≥ 80 %
- [ ] Integration test in staging for 24 h
- [ ] Feature flag verified off-by-default in prod
- [ ] Runbook updated if operational behaviour changes
Three metrics answer whether the train cadence is working. Track them per-train in your observability stack:
Add buffer to next train; root-cause slip
Collect these automatically by querying the GitHub Milestones API after each deploy:

import os, requests, datetime

REPO  = os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"]
TOKEN = os.environ["GH_TOKEN"]
H = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}", "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"}

def train_metrics(milestone_number: int) -> dict:
    ms   = requests.get(f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/milestones/{milestone_number}", headers=H).json()
    open_issues   = ms["open_issues"]
    closed_issues = ms["closed_issues"]
    total = open_issues + closed_issues
    scope_accuracy = closed_issues / total if total else 0

    created = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(ms["created_at"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    due     = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(ms["due_on"].replace("Z", "+00:00")) if ms.get("due_on") else None
    mttd_days = (due - created).days if due else None

    return {
        "milestone": ms["title"],
        "scope_accuracy": round(scope_accuracy, 3),
        "cut_rate": round(open_issues / total, 3) if total else 0,
        "mttd_days": mttd_days,
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import json
    m = train_metrics(milestone_number=42)
    print(json.dumps(m, indent=2))
    # {"milestone": "Train 42", "scope_accuracy": 0.92, "cut_rate": 0.08, "mttd_days": 14}
The retro is the mechanism that improves cadence quality over time. Run it within 24 hours of the production deploy while memory is fresh:

# Train 42 — Post-Deploy Retro

**Date:** 2025-08-13
**Facilitator:** @alice
**Attendees:** @alice @bob @carol @dave @eve

## What went well
- Model upgrade canary rollout was smooth — automated P99 alert fired correctly
- Cut-line script gave clear signal; no last-minute scope debates

## What went wrong
- Cache flush on deploy took 8 min (expected < 2 min) — users saw stale responses
- Runbook for cache flush was out of date

## Root causes
- Cache flush script iterates keys sequentially; needs batching
- Runbook update was a manual step that fell through the cracks

## Action items

| Action | Owner | Due |
|--------|-------|-----|
| Batch cache flush (max 1000 keys/s) | @carol | Train 43 kick-off |
| Add runbook update to PR template checklist | @dave | This week |
| Add cache flush duration alert (> 3 min) | @eve | Train 43 |

## Train metrics summary
- Scope accuracy: 92 % (11/12 features)
- Cut rate: 8 % (1 feature slipped — retry telemetry)
- MTTD: 14 days (on target)
- Post-deploy incidents: 1 (cache flush latency, P3)
Integrating Model Version Bumps into the Train
→ a future release) is a first-class train scope item, not a hotfix. Pin the version explicitly and gate it through the train's regression suite:

# src/llm_client.py
import anthropic

# Pin the model version as a module-level constant.
# Update only via a PR with eval results attached.
MODEL = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def complete(prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 1024) -> str:
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

# .github/workflows/model-version-check.yml
# Fails the CI if MODEL constant changes without an eval artifact
name: Model Version Gate

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Detect model version change
        run: |
          OLD=$(git show origin/main:src/llm_client.py | grep '^MODEL' | head -1)
          NEW=$(grep '^MODEL' src/llm_client.py | head -1)
          if [ "$OLD" != "$NEW" ]; then
            echo "Model version changed: $OLD -> $NEW"
            # Require eval artifact in PR description
            PR_BODY=$(gh pr view ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} --json body -q .body)
            if ! echo "$PR_BODY" | grep -q "eval-results"; then
              echo "ERROR: Model version bump requires 'eval-results' link in PR description"
              exit 1
            fi
            echo "Eval artifact found — model bump approved"
          fi
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
decouples delivery rhythm from feature readiness, producing predictable deploys and forcing scope discipline
: kick-off → mid-train sync → cut-line meeting → code freeze → smoke gate → deploy → retro
mechanically track train scope; automated labelling at cut-line removes ambiguity
: scope accuracy (≥90%), cut rate (<15%), MTTD (≤14 days)
are first-class train items gated by eval artifact — never casual hotfixes
within 24 hours converts incidents into action items before context fades
feed back into the next kick-off scope document, closing the improvement loop
